sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Guidelines for Virtual Management of Dysuria
sources.population: People presenting with dysuria in telehealth, with age, sex, pregnancy, comorbidity, recurrence, and complicated-infection qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Telehealth services in DHA-licensed health facilities.
sources.applicability_note: UAE-direct for selected dysuria history, red-flag prompts, urinalysis, culture, and qualified renal-testing documentation. Physical examination, diagnosis, medicine, treatment, referral urgency, and non-telehealth pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Scope, purpose, and applicability
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines DHA-licensed telehealth dysuria scope and qualified applicability.

exact_sections.heading: Clinical history for dysuria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports onset, persistence, severity, duration, location, fever, rigors, nausea, vomiting, flank pain, medicine or procedure context, pregnancy and sexual context, trauma or irritants, haematuria, frequency, urgency, and voiding symptoms.

exact_sections.heading: Red flags and referral criteria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports fever with flank pain or tenderness, vomiting, visible haematuria, pregnancy, diabetes, immunosuppression, and recurrence as prompts only, without automatic urgency or referral output.

exact_sections.heading: Investigations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urinalysis, recommendation-qualified urine culture, and renal-function or electrolyte review in qualified pyelonephritis, nausea, or vomiting contexts.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Lower urinary tract symptoms in men: management
sources.version: NICE CG97; last updated 2015-06-03; last reviewed 2024-12-19
sources.population: Men aged 18 years and over with lower urinary tract symptoms, with initial, specialist, complication, and retention qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial and specialist assessment, conservative and medicine management, referral, and urinary-retention care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for adult male LUTS and urinary-retention documentation. The unrestricted legacy workflows do not encode sex or every complication qualifier; treatment, medicine choices, catheter care, surgery, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Initial assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports symptom and medicine history, abdominal and genital examination, digital rectal examination, frequency-volume context, urinalysis, qualified PSA discussion, qualified creatinine, conservative-advice documentation, and referral criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Specialist assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports specialist history and examination, urinary flow, post-void residual, and recommendation-qualified imaging or cystoscopy context.

exact_sections.heading: Treating urinary retention
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports acute or chronic urinary-retention context, palpable or percussable bladder, residual volume, creatinine, qualified imaging, and clinician-led follow-up or referral; catheter and medicine instructions are not mapped.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse in women: management
sources.version: NICE NG123; minor links and language updates through March 2025
sources.population: Women aged 18 and over with urinary incontinence or overactive-bladder symptoms, with UTI, voiding, referral, and treatment qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial assessment, urine testing, bladder diaries, conservative management, and specialist referral.
sources.applicability_note: Partial for a sex-unspecified frequency or urgency workflow. Exact only for selected female urinary-incontinence or overactive-bladder assessment and clinician-documented conservative-management fields; medicines, procedures, fixed follow-up, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Assessing urinary incontinence — history and physical examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urgency-incontinence or overactive-bladder symptom context, relevant contributing factors, and clinician-performed pelvic-floor assessment in adult women.

exact_sections.heading: Urine testing, residual urine, and bladder diaries
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urine dipstick, qualified culture, post-void residual, and bladder-diary documentation in adult women.

exact_sections.heading: Indications for referral to a specialist service
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led referral documentation for qualified bladder, urethral, voiding, neurological, haematuria, or recurrent unexplained UTI contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Lifestyle and behavioural interventions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting caffeine or fluid discussion and bladder training only when discussed or arranged by the clinician for qualified adult women.
